text: We present a case of robotic assisted laparoscopic excision of deep infiltrating endometriosis involving the ureter. This is a thirty-eight-year-old patient with a significant history of endometriosis causing pelvic pain, dyskinesia and dyspareunia. Her past surgical history includes a previous mammectomy, a hysterectomy for pelvic pain and her last surgery was that of a laparoscopic enterolysis and partial removal of a right ovarian cyst. That surgery was aborted due to extensive scar tissue. Due to persistent pelvic pain, she was referred to our center for further management. The patient elected for definitive surgery with a robotic-assisted bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, enteralysis, ureterolysis, and excision of deep infiltrating endometriosis. Preoperative workup included a pelvic MRI with positive findings of a right-sided pelvic mass consistent with an endometrioma measuring seven point six centimeters and replacing the majority of the right ovary.

The surgeon conducts an initial survey of the pelvis, revealing a normal left ovary and a right ovary encased in adhesions involving small bowel loops and the right pelvic side wall. Dissection begins at the remnant of the right round ligament, developing the paravesical and pararectal spaces, and proceeds towards the adhesions surrounding the right ovarian mass to identify the course of the right ureter.

The surgeon performs careful dissection of the small bowel surrounding the right ovary, resulting in the rupture of the right ovarian cyst with chocolate-colored exudate. The small bowel and mesentery are freed from the right ovary, and the dissection continues distally to release the sigmoid colon and proximal rectum from dense adhesions.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal window between the right infundibulopelvic ligament and the stented right ureter, facilitating its identification and dissection. The right infundibulopelvic ligament is cauterized and transected. Adhesiolysis is performed to free the diseased ovary from the right pelvic side wall, preparing for oophorectomy. Extensive fibrosis is observed around the right ureter, which is elevated, freed circumferentially, and traced towards its entry into the bladder. A fibrotic nodule induced by endometriosis is highlighted.

The surgeon performs cold shear dissection along the ureter amidst dense fibrosis, carefully preserving the periurethral sheath to maintain vascular supply. The dissection progresses distally, successfully freeing the ureter down to the bladder.

The surgeon completes the ureterolysis procedure, with a double J stent placed in the right ureter to prevent post-operative stricture formation. Despite the presence of a considerable fibrotic nodule around the ureter, no hydronephrosis is observed.

The patient was discharged on post-op day one. The final pathology was consistent with endometriosis. She had complete resolution of her symptoms at her post-op visit. Endometriosis involving the ureter can make ureterolysis extremely difficult. Despite its incessant recalcitrant nature, endometriosis can be effectively resected to afford a favorable outcome for patients.
